title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a man called Neil talking about a community repair café. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a man called Neil talking about a community repair café. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A man called Neil introduces a community repair café.
extracts.audio_script: Good morning. I’m Neil, and I coordinate Fix-It Repair Café, where people bring damaged household items and learn whether they can be repaired rather than thrown away. We began in a classroom at the adult education college, but moving equipment there every month was difficult. The project now takes place on the ground floor of an empty [9]department store[/9], whose owner lets community groups use the space while the upper floors are being redesigned. It gives us room for several repair tables and a waiting area with drinks.||People bring all sorts of objects. Some arrive with clothes needing a new button, while others carry bicycles or small appliances. The items brought in most frequently at present are [10]table lamps[/10], often because a wire or switch has failed rather than because the whole object is unusable. Visitors do not hand over an item and leave; they watch or assist, so they understand the repair. Before volunteers work with anything electrical, it must pass an initial [11]safety check[/11] carried out by one of our trained team members. Items considered unsafe are not repaired at the café.||New volunteers do not need to know how to mend everything. In fact, we ask them to begin by organising the shared [12]tool boxes[/12], checking that commonly used items are returned after each repair. People with specialist abilities can join a particular table. At the moment, we especially need volunteers who are confident repairing [13]sewing machines[/13], as more visitors have started bringing these in since they began making clothes or curtains at home. All volunteers receive guidance, and nobody is asked to deal with an unfamiliar task alone.||We try to reuse parts where possible, but we also need new materials. A local hardware shop kindly donates some [14]spare parts[/14], such as plugs and small screws, which allows many repairs to remain free. To keep waiting times manageable, visitors with large items should complete an [15]online booking form[/15] before attending, including a description and photograph. People with simple repairs are welcome to arrive without booking, although they may have to wait. We offer short learning sessions too. Last season our session on fixing torn clothes was very popular; this autumn the most requested class is [16]bicycle maintenance[/16].||The café does not charge for repairs, although donations are appreciated. Volunteers completing ten sessions are given a [17]shopping voucher[/17] by a local business as thanks for their time. More importantly, they gain useful practical and communication skills. Finally, not everyone can come to our regular café, especially older people with large household items. Money raised at our December sale will support a new [18]home visit service[/18], allowing experienced volunteers to assess simple repairs in people's homes. We are not able to repair gas equipment or major appliances, but the service should help with many ordinary problems. Application forms for volunteers are available at the front table.

dialogue.speaker: neil
dialogue.text: Good morning. I’m Neil, and I coordinate Fix-It Repair Café, where people bring damaged household items and learn whether they can be repaired rather than thrown away. We began in a classroom at the adult education college, but moving equipment there every month was difficult. The project now takes place on the ground floor of an empty department store, whose owner lets community groups use the space while the upper floors are being redesigned. It gives us room for several repair tables and a waiting area with drinks.||People bring all sorts of objects. Some arrive with clothes needing a new button, while others carry bicycles or small appliances. The items brought in most frequently at present are table lamps, often because a wire or switch has failed rather than because the whole object is unusable. Visitors do not hand over an item and leave; they watch or assist, so they understand the repair. Before volunteers work with anything electrical, it must pass an initial safety check carried out by one of our trained team members. Items considered unsafe are not repaired at the café.||New volunteers do not need to know how to mend everything. In fact, we ask them to begin by organising the shared tool boxes, checking that commonly used items are returned after each repair. People with specialist abilities can join a particular table. At the moment, we especially need volunteers who are confident repairing sewing machines, as more visitors have started bringing these in since they began making clothes or curtains at home. All volunteers receive guidance, and nobody is asked to deal with an unfamiliar task alone.||We try to reuse parts where possible, but we also need new materials. A local hardware shop kindly donates some spare parts, such as plugs and small screws, which allows many repairs to remain free. To keep waiting times manageable, visitors with large items should complete an online booking form before attending, including a description and photograph. People with simple repairs are welcome to arrive without booking, although they may have to wait. We offer short learning sessions too. Last season our session on fixing torn clothes was very popular; this autumn the most requested class is bicycle maintenance.||The café does not charge for repairs, although donations are appreciated. Volunteers completing ten sessions are given a shopping voucher by a local business as thanks for their time. More importantly, they gain useful practical and communication skills. Finally, not everyone can come to our regular café, especially older people with large household items. Money raised at our December sale will support a new home visit service, allowing experienced volunteers to assess simple repairs in people's homes. We are not able to repair gas equipment or major appliances, but the service should help with many ordinary problems. Application forms for volunteers are available at the front table.
